questions.question:
A random variable X is said to be distributed as Bernoulli(theta), denoted by X ~ Bernoulli(theta), if P(X = 1) = theta and P(X = 0) = 1 - theta for 0 < theta < 1. Let Y = (1/300) * sum_{i=1}^{300} X_i, where X_1, X_2, ..., X_300 are independent and identically distributed Bernoulli(theta) random variables with theta = 0.25. The value of P(Y <= 0.3), after approximation through the Central Limit Theorem, is given by (Recall that phi(z) = (1 / sqrt(2*pi)) * integral from -inf to z of e^{-t^2/2} dt):

A. phi(2)
B. phi(1) - phi(-2)
C. phi(-2)
D. phi(0)
questions.to_find: P(Y <= 0.3) using the Central Limit Theorem approximation, with Y = sample mean of 300 iid Bernoulli(0.25) variables

understand.plain: Y is the sample mean of 300 independent Bernoulli(0.25) random variables. By the Central Limit Theorem, Y is approximately normally distributed with mean theta = 0.25 and variance theta*(1-theta)/n = 0.25*0.75/300. To find P(Y <= 0.3), we standardise by subtracting the mean and dividing by the standard deviation, which converts the probability into a standard normal CDF value phi(z). The standardised value z = (0.3 - 0.25) / sqrt(0.25*0.75/300) equals 2, so the answer is phi(2).

keywords.explain: A discrete distribution taking value 1 with probability theta and 0 with probability 1-theta. Its mean is theta and variance is theta*(1-theta).
keywords.example: A fair coin flip: theta = 0.5, so E[X] = 0.5, Var[X] = 0.25.

keywords.explain: For large n, the sample mean of n iid random variables with mean mu and variance sigma^2 is approximately Normal(mu, sigma^2/n), regardless of the original distribution.
keywords.example: Sum of 300 iid Bernoulli(0.25) variables, divided by 300, is approximately Normal(0.25, 0.25*0.75/300).

keywords.explain: phi(z) = P(Z <= z) where Z ~ Normal(0,1). Defined as the integral of the standard normal density from -inf to z.
keywords.example: P(Y <= 0.3) = P(Z <= 2) = phi(2) after standardising Y with the CLT approximation.

understand.visual_alt: Bell curve showing normal approximation of Y with mean 0.25; shaded region to the left of 0.3 labelled phi(2).

given.aim: Compute P(Y <= 0.3) using the CLT approximation, where Y is the sample mean of 300 iid Bernoulli(0.25) variables

terms.meaning: Each X_i takes value 1 with probability 0.25 and value 0 with probability 0.75.
terms.connects: These are the building blocks for the sample mean Y.

terms.meaning: Y is the sample mean of 300 iid Bernoulli observations; by CLT, Y ~ approx Normal(theta, theta*(1-theta)/n).
terms.connects: CLT standardisation converts P(Y <= 0.3) to a phi value.

terms.meaning: Z = (Y - mu) / (sigma/sqrt(n)) ~ Normal(0,1). Here mu = 0.25, sigma^2 = 0.1875, n = 300.
phi(z) = integral from -inf to z of (1/sqrt(2*pi)) * e^{-t^2/2} dt, i.e., P(Z <= z).
terms.connects: The final answer is expressed as phi(2).

given.plan: Step 1: Find E[Y] = theta = 0.25 and Var[Y] = theta*(1-theta)/300. Step 2: Standardise P(Y <= 0.3) by computing z = (0.3 - 0.25)/sqrt(Var[Y]). Step 3: Express the result as phi(z).

steps.title: Mean and variance of the Bernoulli distribution
steps.formula_raw: For X ~ Bernoulli(theta): E[X] = theta, Var[X] = theta*(1-theta)
steps.note: These parameters drive the CLT approximation for the sample mean.

steps.title: Mean and variance of the sample mean Y by CLT
steps.formula_raw: For Y = (1/n) * sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i iid with mean mu and variance sigma^2: E[Y] = mu, Var[Y] = sigma^2/n, and Y ~ approx Normal(mu, sigma^2/n) for large n
steps.note: n = 300 is large enough for the CLT approximation to be valid.

steps.title: Standardise and compute the probability
steps.formula_raw: P(Y <= a) = P(Z <= (a - mu) / (sigma/sqrt(n))) = phi((a - mu) / (sigma/sqrt(n)))

questions.formula_note: New formulas created for Bernoulli mean and variance, CLT sample mean distribution, and standard normal CDF probability conversion.

questions.question:
Four fair coins are tossed simultaneously. The probability that at least one head and one tail turn up is:

A. 1/16
B. 1/4
C. 7/8
D. 15/16
questions.to_find: P(at least one head and at least one tail when four fair coins are tossed)

understand.plain: When four fair coins are tossed, the complementary event to getting at least one head and at least one tail is getting all heads or all tails. Using the complement rule, we subtract both extreme outcomes from 1. There are 2^4 = 16 equally likely outcomes, of which only HHHH and TTTT fail the condition. So the probability is 1 - 2/16 = 14/16 = 7/8.

keywords.explain: P(event) = 1 - P(complement of event). Used to simplify calculations when the complement is easier to compute.
keywords.example: P(at least one H and one T) = 1 - P(all H or all T) = 1 - P(all H) - P(all T).

keywords.explain: For n fair coin tosses there are 2^n total outcomes each with probability (1/2)^n. For 4 coins: 16 outcomes each with probability 1/16.
keywords.example: HHHH has probability (1/2)^4 = 1/16; TTTT also has probability 1/16.

keywords.explain: The number of heads in n fair coin tosses follows Binomial(n, 0.5). P(k heads) = C(n,k) * (1/2)^n.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 420 190' width='420' height='190'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='420' height='190' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>4 Fair Coins: 16 Equally Likely Outcomes</text><rect x='20' y='40' width='78' height='50' rx='6' class='no' opacity='0.7'/><text x='59' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' style='fill:#fff'>HHHH</text><text x='59' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:#fff'>1 outcome</text><rect x='110' y='40' width='200' height='50' rx='6' class='ok' opacity='0.8'/><text x='210' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' style='fill:#fff'>Mixed (H and T)</text><text x='210' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:#fff'>14 outcomes</text><rect x='322' y='40' width='78' height='50' rx='6' class='no' opacity='0.7'/><text x='361' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' style='fill:#fff'>TTTT</text><text x='361' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:#fff'>1 outcome</text><text x='59' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>P = 1/16</text><text x='210' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>P = 14/16 = 7/8</text><text x='361' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>P = 1/16</text><text x='210' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P(at least one H and one T) = 1 - 1/16 - 1/16 = 7/8</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Bar diagram showing 16 outcomes split into HHHH (1), mixed (14), TTTT (1); the 14 mixed outcomes give probability 7/8.

given.aim: Find P(at least one head and at least one tail) when 4 fair coins are tossed simultaneously

terms.meaning: Each coin is independent with P(H) = P(T) = 1/2. Total sample space has 2^4 = 16 equally likely outcomes.
terms.example: Outcomes range from HHHH to TTTT with 14 mixed outcomes in between.

terms.meaning: Exactly one outcome out of 16 where all coins show heads.
terms.connects: This is one part of the complement to the required event.

terms.meaning: Exactly one outcome out of 16 where all coins show tails.
terms.connects: Events A and B are mutually exclusive and together form the complement of the required event.

terms.meaning: P(at least one H and one T) = 1 - P(all H) - P(all T) since the complement is the union of two mutually exclusive extreme outcomes.

given.plan: Use the complement rule: P(at least one H and one T) = 1 - P(all H) - P(all T). Compute each probability using the binomial formula, then subtract from 1.

steps.title: Count total outcomes and the complement
steps.formula_raw: P(event) = (number of favorable outcomes) / (total number of equally likely outcomes)
steps.apply: Total outcomes = 2^4 = 16. The complement event (NOT having at least one H and one T) consists of: all heads (HHHH) = 1 outcome, all tails (TTTT) = 1 outcome. So complement has 2 outcomes.
steps.note: These two outcomes are mutually exclusive.

steps.title: Apply the complement rule
steps.formula_raw: P(E) = 1 - P(E complement) = 1 - P(all H) - P(all T)

solution.result: P(at least one head and at least one tail) = 7/8.

questions.formula_note: Existing formulas probability-equally-likely and complement-rule from the registry are sufficient; no new formulas needed.

formulas.name: Bernoulli Distribution: Mean and Variance
formulas.plain: For X ~ Bernoulli(theta): E[X] = theta, Var[X] = theta*(1-theta)

formulas.name: Central Limit Theorem for Sample Mean
formulas.plain: For n iid random variables X_i with mean mu and variance sigma^2, the sample mean Y = (1/n)*sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i satisfies: E[Y]=mu, Var[Y]=sigma^2/n, and Y is approximately Normal(mu, sigma^2/n) for large n.
formulas.concept: Y_n = (1/n)*sum X_i => (Y_n - mu)/(sigma/sqrt(n)) -> Normal(0,1) in distribution as n -> inf

formulas.name: Standard Normal CDF and Probability Conversion
formulas.plain: P(Y <= a) = phi((a - mu) / (sigma/sqrt(n))) where phi(z) is the standard normal CDF.
formulas.concept: Standardise: Z = (Y - mu)/(sigma/sqrt(n)) ~ Normal(0,1); P(Y<=a) = P(Z<=(a-mu)/(sigma/sqrt(n))) = phi(z)
